paper_title: Efficient Image Synthesis with Sphere Latent Encoder

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Few-step image generation has seen rapid progress, with consistency and meanflow-based methods significantly reducing the number of sampling steps. Despite their low inference cost, these approaches often suffer from training instability and limited scalability. Sphere Encoder is a recent alternative that produces high-quality images in only a few steps; however, it requires repeated transitions between the pixel space and latent space during inference while jointly optimizing reconstruction and generation within a single architecture. This design leads to computational inefficiency and objective conflict between reconstruction and generation. To address these limitations, we decouple the framework into a fixed pretrained image encoder and a separate latent denoising model trained entirely in a spherical latent space. Our approach eliminates repeated pixel-space operations during training and inference, improving efficiency and allowing reconstruction and generation to specialize independently. On Animal-Faces, Oxford-Flowers and ImageNet-1K datasets, our method significantly outperforms Sphere Encoder in both generation quality and inference speed, while achieving competitive results against strong few-step and multi-step baselines.

sections.title: Efficient Image Synthesis with Sphere Latent Encoder
sections.content: Tung Do Thuan Hoang Nguyen Hao Li Mohamed Bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence, UAE {tung.do,thuan.nguyen,hao.li}@mbzuai.ac.ae Project Page: sphere-latent-encoder.github.io m - e F - w I e - 1 Figure 1: Generated samples by Sphere Latent Encoder in 4 steps on Animal-Faces [2], OxfordFlowers [27], and ImageNet-1K [3] (256 256).

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Few-step image generation has seen rapid progress, with consistency and meanflowbased methods significantly reducing the number of sampling steps. Despite their low inference cost, these approaches often suffer from training instability and limited scalability. Sphere Encoder is recent alternative that produces high-quality images in only few steps; however, it requires repeated transitions between the pixel space and latent space during inference while jointly optimizing reconstruction and generation within single architecture. This design leads to computational inefficiency and objective conflict between reconstruction and generation. To address these limitations, we decouple the framework into fixed pretrained image encoder and separate latent denoising model trained entirely in spherical latent space. Our approach eliminates repeated pixel-space operations during training and inference, improving efficiency and allowing reconstruction and generation to specialize independently. On Animal-Faces, Oxford-Flowers and ImageNet-1K datasets, our method significantly outperforms Sphere Encoder in both generation quality and inference speed, while achieving competitive results against strong few-step and multi-step baselines.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Flow Matching [21, 1, 23] provides principled framework for transforming prior distribution into target data distribution by learning transport paths between them. Due to its scalability and strong performance, it has been widely adopted in large-scale generative systems such as Stable Diffusion 3 [6], FLUX [18], and Qwen-Image [33]. However, standard flow matching relies on iterative sampling, which requires multiple function evaluations and results in high inference cost. Recent works [32, 31, 24, 8, 9] aim to reduce the number of sampling steps, moving toward few-step or even single-step generation. Despite these advances in inference efficiency, previous work has shown that these few-step models, including Consistency Models [32] and MeanFlow [8] are often unstable and sensitivity to hyperparameters [12], and may even exhibit inherent instability when trained from scratch [16]. Furthermore, the MeanFlow objective introduces conflicting optimization signals, leading to slow convergence and further difficulties in training [42]. Consequently, these challenges limit the scalability and practical applicability of one-step generative models. In contrast, Sphere Encoder [40] represents an alternative to diffusion and flow-matching methods. It adopts an encoderdecoder architecture that jointly models reconstruction and generation, and projects latent representations onto hypersphere instead of relying on KL divergence objective [17]. Combined with tailored training objectives, this design enables generation from pure Gaussian noise in only few steps. With its simplicity, Sphere Encoder offers conceptually different perspective on generative modeling and demonstrates promising potential for efficient generation. However, despite its promise, Sphere Encoder has two important limitations. First, its generation process repeatedly alternates between latent space and pixel space: latent is decoded into an image, then re-encoded into latent space, and this procedure is repeated for multiple sampling steps. These repeated pixellatent transitions introduce substantial computational overhead during both training and inference, reducing the practical efficiency of the method. We illustrate the generation process of Sphere Encoder on the left of Fig. 2. Second, Sphere Encoder jointly optimizes reconstruction and generation within single encoder decoder architecture. Prior work [36, 30] suggests that reconstruction and generation favor different representations. Improving reconstruction does not necessarily improve generation, and design choices that benefit one can harm the other. Sphere Encoder itself exhibits this tension. In particular, lower noise levels improve reconstruction quality, but they also reduce hyperspherical coverage and weaken generative performance. As result, Yue et al. [40] must rely on substantially larger architecture to maintain sample quality. In this work, we build on the spherical latent perspective of Sphere Encoder while removing its dependence on iterative pixel-space refinement. Our key idea is to perform the entire generative process in latent space. Concretely, we use pretrained representation autoencoder [43] as fixed image tokenizer and train separate denoising model directly on spherical latents. During training, supervision is applied purely in latent space. During sampling, the latent is iteratively refined only in latent space, and the decoder is invoked once at the end to map the final latent back to pixels. This decoupled design eliminates repeated encoding and decoding operations, separates reconstruction from generation, and allows each component to specialize in its own role. Our approach combines the strengths of pretrained latent representations with the efficiency of few-step latent generation. Compared with the original Sphere Encoder, it offers simpler and more efficient pipeline, while preserving the benefits of spherical latent modeling. Compared with few-step diffusion and flow-based methods, it avoids first-order approximation objectives (e.g., Jacobian-vector products) and instead learns direct latent denoising process on structured spherical manifold. As result, the method is both practically efficient and straightforward to optimize. In Fig. 2, we highlight the key difference between Sphere Encoder [40] and our method. 2 Figure 2: Comparison between Sphere Encoder and our method. Left: Sphere Encoder repeatedly alternates between pixel space and latent space during generation, requiring iterative encoding and decoding steps. Right: our method performs denoising entirely in latent space and applies the decoder only once at the end, leading to simpler and more efficient pipeline. We evaluate our method on Animal-Faces [2], Oxford-Flowers [27] and ImageNet-1K [3] datasets following prior work. Experiments show that our latent-only spherical framework substantially improves over Sphere Encoder in both generation quality and inference efficiency, while remaining simple and scalable. In summary, our main contributions are as follows: 1. We introduce Sphere Latent Encoder, generative framework operating in spherical latent space that enables efficient training and sampling without repeated transitions between pixel and latent representations. Our method reduces inference cost by approximately 85% (about 6.5 fewer FLOPs) compared to Sphere Encoder at the same sampling step. 2. We decouple reconstruction and generation by leveraging pretrained representation autoencoder [43] as fixed image tokenizer and training separate denoising model, allowing each component to specialize in its respective task. 3. We conduct extensive experiments on Animal-Faces [2], Oxford-Flowers [27] and ImageNet1K [3], showing that our method significantly outperforms Sphere Encoder and competes with other baselines in both generation quality and sampling speed.

sections.title:
2 Related Works
Efficient Image Generation. Recent works [8, 9, 13, 42] explore training few-step flow-matching
models from scratch. While these methods achieve strong performance in class-conditional generation,
their scalability remains limited due to high training costs and instability in Jacobian–vector product
(JVP) computations. These limitations make it challenging to scale this line of work to larger
settings. Meanwhile, distillation-based methods [38, 26, 37] remain the most practical solution and
are widely adopted in text-to-image generation. Recent work [15] further extends this paradigm by
incorporating reinforcement learning to enable more preference-aware and controllable distillation.
Another emerging direction is drifting models [4]. Although initial results are promising, their
scalability remains limited, as they are prone to mode collapse and often require large batch sizes
during training.
sections.content: Sampling in Autoencoder Latent Spaces. Variational autoencoders [17] match the latent distribution to Gaussian prior, typically using unimodal Gaussian posterior. Increasing network depth improves posterior estimation, but does not change the distribution family. Even with full covariance, it remains unimodal and cannot capture multi-modal structure. This mismatch leads to suboptimal inference and weak latent representations. In addition, an overly expressive decoder can lead to posterior collapse, in which the model ignores the latent variables [19]. As result, standard VAEs struggle to produce high-quality samples directly from the prior. Prior work addresses these issues using hierarchical VAEs or diffusion models, which improve generative performance through multi-step refinement [11]. Sphere Encoder [40] addresses this limitation by projecting latent representations onto hypersphere using RMSNorm [41], encouraging more uniform latent structure and enabling efficient few-step generation from Gaussian noise. Trade-off between reconstruction and generation. [36] shows that increasing the capacity of the visual tokenizer improves reconstruction quality; however, this makes generation harder, requiring larger denoisers and more training to reach similar sample quality. [36, 35] pointed out that this is trade-off between reconstruction and generation, where better reconstruction does not necessarily lead 3 to better generation. RAE [43] further suggests that strong semantic latent representations are crucial for generative performance. At the same time, training primarily with reconstruction objective can produce weak semantical features, resulting in poor generation quality. Sphere Encoder [40] also observes trade-off between generation and reconstruction: lower noise improves reconstruction but degrades generation, as the resulting latents do not sufficiently cover the hypersphere.

sections.title: 3 Background
sections.content: We briefly review the Sphere Encoder [40] by first introducing the intuition behind its objective design, and then outlining its limitations. Spherification function and additive noise. Given an input image RHW 3, the encoder maps the image to latent representation Rhwd, while the decoder reconstructs the image from the latent where = H/P , = W/P , is the channel dimension, and denotes the patch size. spherification function first flattens and projects it onto hypersphere via RMSNorm [41], yielding = (z). To facilitate training, the latent representation is perturbed with two levels of Gaussian noise, producing vNOISY and vnoisy: vNOISY = F(v + σϵ), vnoisy = F(v + σsubϵ) (1) where σ U(0, σmax) and σsub U(0, σ 0.5), where σmax is predefined hyperparameter. This noise injection encourages the latents to densely populate the hyperspherical space, enabling the decoder to learn smooth and continuous mapping over the latent manifold rather than relying on discrete set of embeddings. To supervise the encoder and decoder, three loss functions are introduced, each serving distinct purpose. Pixel reconstruction loss ensures faithful reconstruction between pixel and latent spaces. It combines ℓ1 and LPIPS losses applied between the reconstructed image from vnoisy and the input: Lpixel_recon = LL1+LPIPS (cid:0)D(vnoisy), x(cid:1). Pixel consistency loss enforces consistency between reconstructions from different noise levels, promoting semantic stability: Lpixel_con = LL1+LPIPS (cid:0)D(vNOISY), sg(D(vnoisy))(cid:1). Latent consistency loss encourages the encoder to map distorted or off-manifold reconstructions back to clean latent representations, improving stability during iterative generation. Specifically, the reconstruction from vNOISY is re-encoded, and cosine similarity loss is minimized against the clean latent: Llat_con = Lcos (cid:0)v, E(D(vNOISY))(cid:1). Total training loss is the weighted combination of three above-mentioned losses where λ1, λ2 and λ3 are the weights for each loss. Ltotal = λ1Lpixel_recon + λ2Lpixel_con + λ3Llat_con. Limitation. During generation process, Sphere Encoder relies on iteratively applying encoder and decoder. Specifically, latent code is sampled from Gaussian distribution, decoded into an image, and then re-encoded back into latent space. This procedure is repeated several times until satisfactory image quality is achieved. While only few iterations (e.g., 48 steps) are typically required, the repeated transitions between latent and pixel spaces introduce significant computational overhead, resulting in inefficient generation. In addition, both prior works [43, 36] and Sphere Encoder [40] reveal an inherent trade-off between reconstruction and generation. This suggests that jointly optimizing reconstruction and generation within single model is suboptimal. Instead, decoupling these objectives into separate stages allows each to be optimized more effectively.

sections.title: 4 Methodology
sections.content: To address these limitations, we perform generation entirely in latent space using pretrained encoderdecoder. This eliminates repeated pixellatent transitions and improves test-time efficiency. Our approach consists of three main components. First, we introduce the overall architecture, which enables efficient few-step generation directly in latent space (Sec. 4.1). Next, we present the training objective, designed to learn denoising and reconstruction within the latent domain (Sec. 4.2, Fig. 3). Finally, we describe simple yet effective sampling procedure that avoids pixellatent transitions, enabling fast generation (Sec. 4.4, Algo. 1). Figure 3: Overview of our Sphere Latent Encoder framework and training objectives.

sections.title: 4.1 Denoising model on Spherical Latent Space
sections.content: To facilitate training in latent space, we adopt pretrained representation autoencoder (RAE) from [43], where the encoder is based on DINOv2 and the decoder is ViT-based model. This autoencoder maps an input image R2562563 to latent representation R1616768. We adopt this design because DINOv2 provides semantically rich representations, while the decoder ensures high-fidelity reconstruction, resulting in structured latent space that captures both semantic and discriminative information. We then introduce transformer-based denoising network operating in the latent space. Following prior work [21, 8, 39], we adopt the SiT architecture [25]. We denote the Euclidean latent as and its spherical projection as v. Specifically, we first corrupt the latent with Gaussian noise, + σϵ, and project it onto hypersphere via normalization function F, yielding = F(z + σϵ). The network takes as input and predicts the clean latent z, which is then decoded by to reconstruct the image. Our formulation is closely related to diffusion models in that it learns to denoise corrupted latent inputs. However, unlike standard diffusion approaches, our method does not condition on the noise level or timestep during either training or inference.

sections.title: 4.2 Training Objective
sections.content: While Sphere Encoder [40] mainly computes losses in pixel space, which incurs high GPU memory consumption, we instead shift all supervision to latent space for improved efficiency. Similarly, we consider two levels of noisy spherical latents, denoted as vNOISY and vnoisy in Eq. (1). Reconstruction loss in Eq. (2) is defined as combination of an ℓ1 loss and cosine similarity loss between the predicted latent G(vnoisy) and the clean latent z. This loss encourages the denoised spherical latent to align with the clean latent representation, thereby facilitating accurate reconstruction from both clean and noisy spherical latents. Lrecon = G(vnoisy) z1 + Lcosine similarity (cid:0)G(vnoisy), z(cid:1) (2) Consistency loss in Eq. (3) combines an ℓ1 loss and cosine similarity loss between predictions obtained from two noise levels, vNOISY and vnoisy. To enforce consistency across noise scales, we treat the prediction from the lower-noise latent as fixed target by applying stop-gradient operator. This encourages the model to align predictions from higher-noise latents with those from lower-noise 5 ones, promoting local smoothness on the hypersphere and ensuring that nearby regions correspond to similar image content. Lcons = G(vNOISY) sg(cid:0)G(vnoisy)(cid:1)1 + Lcosine (cid:0)G(vNOISY), sg(cid:0)G(vnoisy)(cid:1)(cid:1) (3) The two losses above are motivated by the pixel reconstruction and pixel consistency objectives in Sphere Encoder [40]. In our method, however, both objectives are shifted from pixel space to latent space for greater efficiency. With these two losses, our denoising model can generate new samples in just few steps.

sections.title: 4.3 Revisiting Noise Sampling and Training Objectives
sections.content: Noise Distribution. Instead of adopting the sampling strategy used in Sphere Encoder [40], we propose an alternative approach for sampling σ and σsub. While we retain the constraint σ σsub, we modify the underlying sampling procedure. Concretely, for each data point, we draw two independent samples from logit-normal distribution [6, 8]. This distribution is obtained by first sampling (µ, σ2) and then applying the logistic function to map into the interval (0, 1). We assign the larger sample to σ and the smaller one to σsub, inducing joint distribution over (σ, σsub) that differs from the original Sphere Encoder formulation. Sphere Encoder [40] jointly optimizes reconstruction and generation within single framework, so its noise schedule must balance hyperspherical coverage for generation against reconstruction fidelity. This coupling leads to relatively conservative noise sampling strategy. In contrast, our framework decouples reconstruction from generation using fixed pretrained autoencoder and separate latent denoising model, allowing us to adopt more aggressive noise schedules that are better suited to few-step sampling. Removing Latent Consistency Loss. Sphere Encoder [40] introduces latent consistency loss to encourage consistency between decoder and encoder, but this objective adds notable computational overhead during training. We explore simplified variant of this loss in latent space (see Appendix Section B) and observe that removing it improves both performance and training efficiency. Our approach instead uses dedicated denoising model, avoiding the need to impose such consistency constraint and eliminating the latent consistency loss. Our denoising model is trained over broader and smoother noisy latent space on the hypersphere, leading to stronger denoising performance without relying on this objective.

sections.title: 4.4 Sampling in Spherical Latent Space
sections.content: With the proposed loss functions, our model enables multi-step sampling entirely in latent space (see Algo. 1). We begin by sampling latent from Gaussian distribution (Alg. 1, line 1). At each iteration, the latent is projected onto the hypersphere (Alg. 1, line 4) and passed through the denoising model to obtain refined latent via classifier-free guidance (Alg. 1, line 5). Algorithm 1 Iterative Latent Sampling with Classifier-Free Guidance Require: steps , σmax, guidance ω, label 1: (0, I) 2: ϵ (0, I) 3: for = 0 to 1 do F(z) 4: zguided G(v, ) + ω (G(v, y) G(v, )) 5: (cid:0)1 t+1 6: // Spherify and re-noise 7: F(zguided) 8: + ϵ σmax 9: 10: end for 11: decode(z) 12: return (cid:1)γ This guided latent is then re-projected onto the hypersphere (Alg. 1, line 8), perturbed with noise (Alg. 1, line 9), and used as input for the next iteration. We progressively decrease the noise magnitude over iterations (Alg. 1, line 6), following Sphere Encoder [40], so that smaller perturbations are introduced as sampling proceeds. This iterative procedure alternates between projection, denoising, and re-noising, allowing the model to gradually refine the latent and converge to high-quality samples. For detailed hyperparameter settings, please refer to Appendix Section B.

sections.title: 5.1 Few-step Image Generation
sections.content: Evaluation protocol. We evaluate generation quality using the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) [10]. The metric is computed on 50,000 randomly sampled images; for detailed calculation, please refer to Appendix Section B. For class-conditional generation, we adopt balanced sampling strategy by drawing an equal number of samples from each class, following RAE [43]. We conduct experiments on Animal-Faces [2], Oxford-Flowers [27], with all images resized to 256 256, and ImageNet1K [3], where images are first center-cropped to square and then resized to 256 256. We apply minimal data augmentation, consisting only of horizontal flipping with probability of 0.5. For implementation details, please refer to Appendix Section A. Table 1: Quantitative comparison (gFID ) and Computational cost (GFLOP ) on Animal-Faces [2] and Oxford Flowers [27] with different numbers of sampling steps (2, 4, 6). Metric / Method Params 2 6 Params 2 4 6 Animal-Faces [2] Oxford-Flowers [27] FID (gFID ) Sphere Encoder [40] Ours GFLOPs Sphere Encoder [40] Ours 642M 130M 642M 130M 19.29 10. 18.23 6.89 17.97 6.18 1965 302 4554 390 7144 478 948M 130M 948M 130M 16.60 12.22 12.96 8.61 12.26 7.85 3932 390 9118 14300 743 Quantitative results on small datasets. We compare our method against Sphere Encoder [40]. To ensure fair comparison, we report two variants of our approach: one with classifier-free guidance for Oxford-Flowers, and one without classifier-free guidance for Animal-Faces. Quantitative results are presented in Table 1. Our method significantly outperforms prior work by large margin, while using smaller architecture and operating entirely in the latent space during sampling, resulting in substantially lower computational cost in terms of GFLOPs across all sampling steps compared to Sphere Encoder (see Appendix Section for details). (a) Sphere Encoder [40] (b) Ours Figure 4: Qualitative comparison between Sphere Encoder [40] and our method on 4-steps image generation on ImageNet-1K [3]. Quantitative results on ImageNet-1K. We compare our method against Sphere Encoder [40], as well as representative multi-step diffusion and flow matching models [29, 25, 39, 34, 36, 20, 22, 43], and recent few-step generation approaches [31, 7, 8, 42]. As shown in Table 2, our method achieves strong performance while requiring significantly fewer function evaluations. Compared to Sphere Encoder under the same sampling budget (4 2 NFE), our approach reduces FID from 4.02 to 2.25, demonstrating substantial improvement in sample quality, and further improves to 2.11 at 62 NFE. This advantage is also reflected in the qualitative results in Fig. 4, where our method produces sharper images with finer details. While multi-step diffusion and flow-based methods can achieve lower FID scores, they require orders of magnitude more evaluations (e.g., 250 2), highlighting the efficiency of our approach in the low-NFE regime. Moreover, our method attains performance comparable to recent 1-step diffusion and flow-based approaches that rely on first-order approximations via Jacobian-vector products. In contrast, our formulation avoids such approximations, leading to simpler and more stable training process while also yielding improved performance on additional evaluation metrics. We further compare computational cost in terms of FLOPs in Table 5b and show 7 that our method is substantially more efficient than Sphere Encoder even with more sampling steps. Additional comparisons across metrics are provided in Appendix Section C. Table 2: Quantitative comparison of few-step class-conditional image generation on ImageNet-1K [3]. NFE denotes the number of function evaluations; 2 indicates the use of classifier-free guidance. 1-NFE diffusion/flow from scratch Multi-NFE diffusion/flow Method # Params NFE MeanFlow-XL/2 [8] α-Flow-XL/2+ [42] iMF-XL/2 [9] 676M 676M 610M 1 1 1 Sphere model Sphere Encoder [40] Ours-XL/2 Ours-XL/2 1.3B 675M 675M 4 2 4 2 6 2 FID 3.43 2.58 1.72 4.02 2.25 2. Method # Params NFE DiT-XL/2 [29] SiT-XL/2 [25] SiT-XL/2 + REPA [39] SiT-XL/2 + REG [34] LightningDiT-XL/2 [36] REPA-E [20] GAE [22] RAE + DiT-XL [43] 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 675M 839M 250 2 250 2 250 2 250 2 250 2 250 2 250 2 50 FID 2.27 2.06 1.42 1.36 1.35 1.15 1.13 1.

sections.title: 5.2 Ablation Studies
sections.content: We conduct series of ablation studies to systematically analyze the key design choices of our method using the SiT-B/1 architecture [25]. For quantitative evaluation, we construct an ImageNet-100 subset by random sampling 100 classes from ImageNet-1K [3], which provides well-representative yet computationally efficient benchmark. To evaluate the impact of the latent autoencoder, we use only the Animal-Faces dataset [2], as it provides simpler and more controlled setting. We examine five main factors: Noise Distribution, Training Losses, Latent Projection, Inference Steps, and Autoencoder. Table 3: Ablation studies for few-step image generation on ImageNet-100 and Animal-Faces (FID ). Improvement is reported relative to the first setting within each factor. R: reconstruction loss; C: consistency loss; L: latent consistency loss.

sections.title: FID
sections.content: Impr. (%) Noise Distribution Baseline Uniform LogNorm (0.4, 1.0) LogNorm (+0.4, 1.0) ImageNet-100 Training Losses Latent Projection Sampling Steps Animal-Faces Autoencoder indicates continued fine-tuning for 300 epochs. R+C R+C+L R+C w/o spherify spherify 2 4 8 Flux VAE [18] GAE [22] RAE [43] 6.43 5.79 5.56 5.31 8.97 5.31 4.82 4.68 89.68 4.68 12.47 4.90 4. 172.25 23.26 10.63 10.0 13.5 17.4 40.8 46.3 47.8 1816.2 60.7 66.9 86.5 93. Noise Distribution. We analyze the effect of different noise distributions during training by comparing our variants with the baseline method [40]. Specifically, we evaluate Uniform, LogNorm(0.4, 1.0), and LogNorm(+0.4, 1.0), and report results on ImageNet-100 in Table 3. All proposed distributions outperform the baseline, yielding at least 10.0% improvement in FID. We attribute these gains to the use of dedicated denoising model, which can better handle higher noise levels during training and thus improves generation quality. Among the variants, LogNorm(0.4, 1.0) achieves 13.5% improvement, while LogNorm(+0.4, 1.0) performs best with 17.4% gain. We adopt the latter in all subsequent experiments. Training Objectives. We analyze the impact of the proposed training objectives. Introducing the consistency loss yields substantial improvement of 40.8% over the reconstruction-only baseline, as it enforces proximity between noisy latent pairs and encourages the denoising network to produce 8 outputs that are both clean and semantically aligned with the underlying latent representation. We further incorporate latent consistency loss following the formulation of Sphere Encoder [40] (see Appendix for details); however, this additional constraint degrades performance, resulting in only 46.3% improvement. We hypothesize that, since the model is already trained under strong noise conditions, the extra regularization imposes unnecessary constraints and increases optimization difficulty. Overall, combining reconstruction and consistency losses achieves the best performance, with 47.8% improvement. Latent Projection. We investigate the importance of the spherify function by replacing RMSNorm [41] with an identity mapping. This modification leads to dramatic performance drop, with outputs becoming incoherent, increasing FID from 4.68 to 89.68 in Table 3. The results suggest that the latent denoiser and representation autoencoder cannot function effectively out-of-the-box, and instead require careful design within spherical latent space training framework. Inference Steps. We analyze the effect of the number of inference steps at test time. As shown in Table 3 and Fig. 5a, generation quality improves substantially when increasing the number of steps from 2 to 4, with diminishing gains beyond 4 steps and near saturation at 8 steps. These results suggest that only small number of inference steps is needed to obtain high-quality samples. Choice of Latent Autoencoder. We study the impact of the latent autoencoder using the AnimalFaces dataset [2]. As shown in Table 3 and Fig. 5b, performance strongly depends on the quality and dimensionality of the latent representation. FLUX-VAE [18] performs worst, with FID of 172.25, due to its low latent dimensionality (d = 16), which limits capacity and leads to poorly separated embeddings. GAE [22] improves the representation by incorporating vision foundation models during training, achieving 23.26, but remains constrained by its compact latent space (d = 32). In contrast, RAE [43] achieves the best results, with 10.63, by combining semantically rich features (e.g., DINOv2 [28]) with high-dimensional latent space (d = 768), providing both strong representation quality and sufficient capacity for effective denoising. - 2 - 4 - 8 X [ 1 8 ] E [ 2 2 ] [ 4 3 ] (a) Effect of inference steps on ImageNet-100. (b) Effect of autoencoders on Animal-Faces. Figure 5: Increasing inference steps improves fidelity (a), while stronger semantic representations and larger latent dimensionality yield sharper samples (b).

sections.title: 6 Limitations and Conclusion
sections.content: Our method still has some limitations. First, we focus on class-conditional image generation and do not evaluate harder settings such as text-to-image generation. Second, our approach relies on strong pretrained representation autoencoder to provide semantically meaningful latent representations. This dependency is central to the method and may limit its generality, as performance can vary with the choice and quality of the pretrained encoder, and may introduce additional computational or availability constraints. Third, due to this reliance on fixed encoder, it remains challenging to achieve high-quality one-step sampling; future work on jointly fine-tuning the encoder with representation alignment from vision foundation models may help address this limitation. In conclusion, we propose an efficient image generation framework that operates entirely in spherical latent space. By decoupling reconstruction from generation, our approach avoids repeated pixellatent transitions, resulting in simpler pipeline with improved efficiency and better performance. We hope this work encourages further exploration of spherical latent-space modeling, as well as extensions to more general conditioning settings and challenging generation tasks.

sections.title: A Implementation
sections.content: Table 4: Configurations on different datasets. dataset Animal-Faces [2] Oxford-Flowers [27] ImageNet-1K [3] 676 28 1152 16 600 130 12 768 12 500 256 model configuration params (M) depth hidden dim heads patch size training epochs batch size dropout optimizer lr schedule lr Adam (β1, β2) weight decay ema decay Loss configuration σ, σsub sampler L1 Reconstruction weight L1 Consistency weight Cosine Reconstruction weight Cosine Consistency weight σ range σ mix range Mix probability Cls-cond drop 130 12 768 12 1 2000 256 0.0 AdamW constant 0.0001 (0.9, 0.95) 0.0 0.9995 lognorm(0.4, 1.0) 50.0 25.0 1.0 1.0 [0, 85] [85, 89] 0.2 0.1 Denoising model architecture. We adopt standard transformer-based architecture as the denoising model. The input latent has spatial size 16 16 with 768 channels. The backbone follows the SiT architecture [25]. Detailed configuration for each dataset is provided in Table 4. For experiments on Animal-Faces [2] and Oxford-Flowers [27], we adopt the SiT-B/1 architecture [25] as our denoising model, corresponding to the Base configuration with patch size of 1. For the large-scale ImageNet1K dataset [3], we adopt the SiT-XL/1 architecture [25] as our denoising model with patch size of 1, resulting in comparable number of processing tokens to prior works [8, 39, 9]. Representation autoencoder. We use pretrained representation autoencoder from [43], where the encoder is DINOv2-B [28] and the decoder is ViT-XL. The model contains 502M parameters and requires 128 GFLOPs for forward pass (encoder + decoder), which is substantially lower than SD-VAE (445 GFLOPs). The autoencoder maps input images of size 256 256 3 to latents of size 16 16 768. The reconstruction FID (rFID) reported in [43] is 0.49. Datasets and training details. We conduct experiments on Animal-Faces [2], Oxford Flowers [27], and ImageNet-1K [3]. All experiments are performed at resolution of 256 256, with horizontal flipping applied as the only data augmentation with probability of 0.5. For ImageNet-1K, nonsquare images are first center-cropped to square before resizing. To improve training efficiency, all images are pre-encoded into latent representations using DINOv2-B [28], significantly reducing computational overhead. We train our models on 8 AMD Instinct MI210 GPUs (64 GB memory each) for Animal-Faces and Oxford-Flowers, and scale up to 64 such GPUs for ImageNet-1K. Sampling Configuration. We conduct experiments on Animal-Faces [2], Oxford Flowers [27], and ImageNet-1K [3]. All experiments use resolution of 256 256 with horizontal flipping applied with probability 0.5. For ImageNet-1K, non-square images are center-cropped before resizing. All 13 images are pre-encoded into latent representations using DINOv2-B [28], which significantly reduces training overhead.

sections.title: B Additional Technical Details
sections.content: Latent Consistency Loss. We follow the latent consistency loss in Sphere Encoder [40] and adapt it to latent space. Starting from noisy latent, we obtain refined prediction zrefined = G(vNOISY) and project it onto the hypersphere to obtain vrefined = F(zrefined). We then apply the same noise level σ to produce vre-noisy = F(vrefined + σϵ), which is passed through the model again to obtain zre-refined = G(vre-noisy). Finally, we enforce consistency between the two predictions using cosine similarity loss: Llat_cons = Lcosine (cid:0)zre-refined, zrefined (cid:1) (4) Experiment with latent consistency loss. In early experiments, we observe that including this loss from the beginning of training substantially slows convergence and increases iteration time (from 201 ms to 336 ms per iteration). We hypothesize that, during early training, the denoising model is not yet capable of producing high-quality cleaned latents; consequently, the content of zrefined is unreliable, making the latent consistency objective Llat_cons difficult to optimize. To mitigate this issue, we perform an ablation study where we first train the model for 500 epochs using both the reconstruction loss and the latent consistency loss, and then fine-tune for an additional 300 epochs under two settings: with and without the latent consistency loss. Results are reported in Table 3, showing that removing the latent consistency loss yields better performance. Metric Calculation. We follow RAE [43] and use class-balanced sampling to generate 50,000 images. For ImageNet-1K [3], we adopt the reference statistics from Guided Diffusion [5]. For CMMD [14], we evaluate using the same generated and reference sets. For Animal-Faces [2] and Oxford-Flowers [27] datasets, we use the training set as the reference distribution. Following standard practice, we sample 50,000 images for evaluation, even when the training sets contain fewer samples. FLOP Calculation. We compute the computational cost of all models using the THOP library1, which estimates the number of floating-point operations (FLOPs). All measurements are reported per image with batch size of 1. For our method, the total FLOPs are determined by the number of sampling steps. Each step consists of one forward pass of the denoising model, followed by final decoder pass to reconstruct the image. When classifier-free guidance (CFG) is applied, the FLOPs of the denoising model are doubled at each step. For Sphere Encoder, the generation process alternates between decoder and encoder passes. Consequently, the total FLOPs for sampling steps are computed as decoder passes and (T 1) encoder passes. When CFG is enabled, both encoder and decoder computations are doubled accordingly. All reported FLOPs in Table 1 follow these conventions. We further provide detailed breakdown of FLOPs and parameter counts for each component of our method and Sphere Encoder [40] in Table 5c. Noise perturbation during sampling. Before the beginning of each new sampling cycle, we deliberately perturb the predicted latent following the Sphere Encoder approach [40]. Specifically, we adopt their simple strategy of progressively decaying the injected noise. Across most experiments, we observe that applying decreasing noise schedule at each timestep improves model performance. For sampling on ImageNet-1K [3], we use decay factor of γ = 0.5 to gradually reduce the noise as sampling progresses. For other datasets, including Animal-Faces [2] and Oxford-Flowers [27], we set γ = 0.75. All experiments use the same maximum noise level σmax = 24. We use ω = 1.0 (i.e., no classifier-free guidance) for Animal-Faces, ω = 4, 8 for Oxford-Flowers, and ω = 3.2 for ImageNet-1K. 1https://github.com/ultralytics/thop

sections.title: C Additional Quantitative Results
sections.content: Few-step Image Generation on ImageNet-1K. We further evaluate our method on ImageNet-1K using varying numbers of sampling steps, as shown in Table 5a. The generation quality improves significantly when increasing the number of steps from 2 to 4. However, the improvement becomes marginal beyond 4 steps and nearly saturates at 6 steps. Table 5: ImageNet-1K results and computational cost analysis. (a) Performance of our method across different sampling steps. (b) Total FLOPs comparison with Sphere Encoder during sampling. (c) Per-component parameter count and FLOPs for each method. (a) Performance of our method on ImageNet-1K with different numbers of sampling steps (NFE = 2, 4, 6), evaluated using FID and CMMD. (b) Comparison of computational cost (GFLOPs) between Sphere Encoder [40] and our method at comparable sampling budgets. NFE FID CMMD 2 6.85 0.349 4 2.25 0.144 6 2.11 0. Method NFE FLOPs (G) Sphere Encoder [40] Ours 4 2 6 2 13326 (c) Per-forward-pass parameters and FLOPs of each component used in sampling. Method Component Params FLOPs (G) Sphere Encoder Sphere Encoder Ours Ours Encoder Decoder Denoising model Decoder 682M 702M 674M 415M 918 977 230 213 Performance on Additional Metrics. Since FID [10] can be unreliable due to its Gaussianity assumption, we further evaluate our method using CMMD [14]. As shown in Table 6, our method achieves competitive performance on this metric, outperforming several multi-step flow matching methods [43, 34] as well as the state-of-the-art one-step Improved MeanFlow model [9]. Table 6: Quantitative comparison of class-conditional image generation on ImageNet-1K [3]. Lower FID and CMMD are better (), while higher IS, Precision, and Recall are better ().

sections.title: D Additional Qualitative Results
sections.content: We present 256 256 samples generated by our best-performing model, SiT-XL/1 with DINOv2-B, using classifier-free guidance of 3.2, σmax = 24, and γ = 0.5. In addition, we provide pairwise comparisons between 4-step and 6-step sampling in Fig. 6. The 6-step samples are consistently sharper and exhibit reduced distortions.

sections.title: E Broader Societal Impacts
sections.content: Our method improves the efficiency of image generation, enabling applications in content creation and machine learning while reducing computational cost. However, easier generation of realistic images may increase risks of misuse, such as deceptive or misleading content. We encourage responsible use and further research on safeguards like detection and usage guidelines.
